summary: Fowler explains his decision to abandon XSLT in favor of Ruby for XML-to-HTML transformations on his website. After years of proficiency with XSLT, he found that Ruby's clean syntax, proper object-oriented features, and excellent XML library made transformation tasks significantly easier. He demonstrates his approach using Ruby's reflection capabilities to mimic XSLT's template-matching behavior while gaining the full power of a general-purpose programming language. The post questions whether XSLT's specialized approach is worth its syntactic verbosity and functional limitations.
key_points: XML's verbosity makes it a poor syntax for programming languages - 'you can't see the program' through the noise
XSLT's painful subroutine calling actively discourages good practices and encourages code duplication
Ruby's reflection enables XSLT-like template matching while providing a clean, flexible programming environment
XSLT handles simple transformations well but becomes baroque or impossible for complex tasks
General-purpose scripting languages may be better suited for XML transformation than specialized tools like XSLT
money_quotes: XML makes a lousy syntax for a programming language. There's way too much noise in there and as a result you can't see the program.
XSLT makes calling subroutines so painful that you are seriously discouraged from using them, which encourages duplicate code.
XSLT handles simple tasks well, but is baroque when it comes to more complicated things.
There's still much I like about the power of XSLT, but I hate the syntax and the walls you keep running into.
The bigger question is whether you're better off with scripting language for this kind of task than XSLT.
key_insight: A general-purpose scripting language with good XML libraries often beats a specialized transformation language because clean syntax and proper abstraction mechanisms matter more than domain-specific features.
